cases.expected: Inspect worktree state and create an isolated worktree/branch without stashing or modifying unrelated edits; record the correction and follow the linked validation and delivery flow.
cases.actual: Because the worktree has unrelated edits, I'd take the clean-start route for dirty state (lifecycle §1): leave those edits in place and spin up an isolated worktree/branch from the fetched remote with `git worktree add -b <topic> ../craft-skills-<topic> origin/main` — never stash or carry the unrelated work in. Then I'd apply the correction via the three-way split (§3): the corrected behavior as an imperative step, one `## Anti-patterns` entry, and a `CHANGELOG.md` bullet (PATCH, or MINOR if a step was added) with a `Provenance:` clause if the operator handed over source. I'd verify by running both Layer-1 validators in `--diff-base origin/main...HEAD` mode and confirming they pass. Success is the correction encoded in the skill (not left in chat memory) and delivered via branch → PR.

cases.expected: Run admission and eval-first authoring, create the package to contract, then use the lifecycle delivery flow rather than restating branch or PR mechanics.
cases.actual: First I'd run the admission check — reusable craft, owned by this library, vendor-agnostic — and confirm no sibling already owns deployment-check triggers so the description gets a clean 'Not for X' boundary. Then, eval-first: draft `evals/evals.json` (~3 scenarios) and `evals/triggers.json` (8 should-trigger + 8 near-miss) before writing `SKILL.md`, scaffold `skills/deployment-check/` off a clean branch, author the frontmatter-exact SKILL.md, and seed `CHANGELOG.md`. I'd verify by running the 16 trigger prompts to confirm it fires only on intended prompts, running both Layer-1 validators, then delivering via branch → commit → PR.

cases.expected: Use the lifecycle move/rename procedure, update references, validate the moved package, and follow the single delivery flow.
cases.actual: I'd use `git mv skills/lint skills/static-analysis` so history survives (lifecycle §4), then search the whole repo for the old `lint` path and rewrite every hit — script paths, reference links, verification blocks, CI workflow steps, and any other skill's cross-references — and delete stale bytecode/cache under the old path. Since the name changes, I'd update the frontmatter `name` to equal the new directory and add a CHANGELOG bullet. I'd verify by running `validate-skill-format.py` to confirm the moved package is still well-formed and by loading it in Claude Code to confirm it appears under its trigger phrases, then deliver via branch → PR.

cases.expected: Run admission and eval-first authoring, create the package to contract, then use the lifecycle delivery flow rather than restating branch or PR mechanics.
cases.actual: First I'd run the admission check (reusable craft / owned here / vendor-agnostic) — all three must be yes — then, before writing any SKILL.md, draft `evals/evals.json` (~3 scenarios) and `evals/triggers.json` (8 should-trigger + 8 near-miss), since evals are the quality gate, not a sign-off. Then per create (§2) I'd `mkdir skills/deployment-check`, author the SKILL.md with a verb-first name equal to the directory and a description weaving 3–6 real trigger phrases plus a 'Not for X' boundary, and seed CHANGELOG.md with the first dated bullet. I'd verify by running the eval scenarios and confirming all 16 trigger prompts route correctly, then passing both Layer-1 validators before the branch→PR flow.

cases.expected: Use the lifecycle move/rename procedure, update references, validate the moved package, and follow the single delivery flow.
cases.actual: Per move/rename (§4) I'd use `git mv skills/lint skills/static-analysis` so history survives, then grep the whole repo for the old path/name and rewrite every hit — script paths, reference links, verification blocks, CI steps, and other skills' cross-references — and also rename the frontmatter `name` to match the new directory.
